fact: January 10 is exactly 10 days into the new year, which means you've already lived through 1.4% of the entire year!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when many squirrels start getting super cranky because their buried nuts are frozen solid underground.
The sun rises about 1 minute earlier each day around January 10 in most places, so daylight is slowly creeping back after the winter solstice.
January 10 falls smack in the middle of Capricorn season, when people born under this zodiac sign are supposedly extra stubborn and goal-obsessed.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, January 10 is peak summer, so kids are probably sweating through their summer vacation right now.
Tree sap moves so slowly on cold January days that maple trees in Canada are basically taking a months-long nap.
Why do your boogers freeze instantly when you breathe outside? January 10 is prime nose-icicle season in cold climates!
The moon phase on January 10 changes every year, but it's always somewhere in its monthly journey of getting fatter or skinnier in the sky.
Penguins in Antarctica are busy feeding their fluffy gray chicks around this time, regurgitating fishy meals that would make you gag.
January 10 is roughly 20 days after the winter solstice, which means we're still in the 'dead of winter' phase when everything feels extra cold and sleepy.
In northern countries, the snow on January 10 makes a different crunching sound than December snow because it's had more time to freeze into harder crystals.
Bears in hibernation have been asleep for about a month by January 10, and their heart rate has slowed down to only 8 beats per minute.
